title: [Bug]: `--add-host` option of `podman build` command is not effective in the created container
body:
### Issue Description

Hi team, I have a feeling that I'm doing something stupid, but I'm not able to understand what I am missing. I'm trying to use the `--add-host` option with the `podman build` command but it has no effect on the container that is then being built based on the produced image. The DOC states that this option should add an entry to the _/etc/hosts_ file, but I don't observe that behavior. The same switch used with the `podman run` command works as expected, so I'm surprised the option used in both commands is not giving similar results.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

`podman_bug_reporting # cat Dockerfile`
FROM alpine:latest
RUN echo "I do not need this line"
`podman_bug_reporting # podman image build --add-host=build_example_host:192.168.100.100 -t bug_example_image .`
STEP 1/2: FROM alpine:latest
Resolved "alpine" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/000-shortnames.conf)
Trying to pull docker.io/library/alpine:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob sha256:a9eaa45ef418e883481a13c7d84fa9904f2ec56789c52a87ba5a9e6483f2b74f
Copying config sha256:04eeaa5f8c35b8206a15c12425498c37ecd2181e0ef202bea24a2382e4dd240f
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
STEP 2/2: RUN echo "I do not need this line"
I do not need this line
COMMIT bug_example_image
--> a75d9592534
Successfully tagged localhost/bug_example_image:latest
a75d95925349d29d92076310f36f5e28516f2dd19ec57495ce898588c64ddf3f
`podman_bug_reporting # podman run -d -t bug_example_image`
8014fe73a3820769fd45e7c330e0f77a9bf1a7a85a24a83ee582da6935ff77e1
`podman_bug_reporting # podman exec -it 8014fe73a3820769fd45e7c330e0f77a9bf1a7a85a24a83ee582da6935ff77e1 /bin/sh`
/ # cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1	localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1	localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
10.88.0.2	8014fe73a382 relaxed_golick
/ # ^C
/ # 
`podman_bug_reporting # podman run -d -t --add-host=run_example_host:192.168.100.100 bug_example_image`
200c64094cdc1ee8e55bf0d2916ad5dceefecaa3df5c904b6142325765185c77
`podman_bug_reporting # podman exec -it 200c64094cdc1ee8e55bf0d2916ad5dceefecaa3df5c904b6142325765185c77 /bin/sh`
/ # cat /etc/hosts
**192.168.100.100	run_example_host**
127.0.0.1	localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1	localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
10.88.0.3	200c64094cdc admiring_lamport
/ # ^C
/ # 

### Describe the results you received

The output of the `cat /etc/hosts` command executed in the first container does not contain any custom entry, the output of the same command executed in the second container contains only the entry added with the `podman run` command:
**192.168.100.100	run_example_host**

### Describe the results you expected

The output of the `cat /etc/hosts` command executed in both the containers should include the following line:
**192.168.100.100	build_example_host**

### Additional environment details

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
`--add-host` adds the entry to the build container, i.e. it is only there during your RUN statements. It is never added in the final image. It wouldn't make sense either since podman and docker by default do not use the /etc/hosts file from the image.

```
$ podman build --add-host test:10.0.0.1 .
STEP 1/2: FROM alpine
STEP 2/2: RUN cat /etc/hosts
10.0.0.1        test
127.0.0.1       localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1     localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6
127.0.0.1       host1
127.0.0.1       host2
192.168.188.25  host.containers.internal
10.0.2.100      c2a92f803d80 alpine-working-container
COMMIT
--> 8250eea3f65
8250eea3f6500cbb0b4fd50341b65785bcd1ea627ef11a2fc87ae75b886911c9
$ cat Containerfile 
FROM alpine
RUN cat /etc/hosts
```

OK, thanks for the explanation. In short, my followup question would be: how then add a custom _/etc/hosts_ entry automatically to every container built from specific image?

In the service I use I don't have any control over the `podman/docker run` statement execution - I'm only providing the image to be used (by means of _docker.io_ repository details) and then I'm presented with a result of running my image by the service.

The problem is, the networking is not correctly set up and the container created from my image cannot resolve the hostname I'm trying to connect to from that container. The IP seems to be visible for it, but unfortunately the server certificate used in secure communication is created based on the hostname of the server I'm connecting to, so when I'm using the IP address instead, the connection cannot be established due to the certificate issue. If I do `podman exec -it <name> /bin/bash` into my container and manually add the entry to the _/etc/hosts_ file, the secure connection can be established successfully, so I'm looking for a way to ensure the hostname of the server is always resolvable out-of-the-box when the container is being created from my image. My first guess was to add `RUN echo "IP hostname" > /etc/hosts` to the Dockerfile but I've learnt that podman is in charge of the _/etc/hosts_ file so I thought the `--add-host` option of `podman build` is exactly what I was looking for (as I'm looking for a way to get the same result as if I would add `--add-host` option to `podman run` command but already encode this custom _/etc/hosts_ file entry during the image creation).

By default podman uses entries from the /etc/hosts on the host so you could also add you desired entry there. 

Alternatively, there is a containers.conf option `base_hosts_file` that can be used to overwrite hosts entries.
https://github.com/containers/common/blob/main/docs/containers.conf.5.md#containers-table

I think you can set this option to `"image"` and then it will actually add the entries to the images hosts file during build and when run is used it will also use the entries from there.

NOTE: Because you use podman machine, this conf file must be changed in the VM, i.e. podman machine ssh.
OK, thanks for the extended help, I appreciate that.
